description:
You are acting as a system administrator who keeps a small fleet of non-privileged servers tidy with simple, user-level cron jobs.

Goal  
Create a brand-new personal cron entry (i.e., under the current **user** account, **not** system-wide) that will run one of your maintenance scripts once a day.  
The resulting cron entry must:

1. Execute the script  
   /home/user/backup/cleanup.sh  
2. Run every day at **03:30** system time.  
3. Append both standard output and standard error of the script to  
   /home/user/backup/cleanup.log  
   (use the usual Bash redirection ‘>> … 2>&1’).  
4. Be the **only** new line you add; do not remove or modify any pre-existing cron lines if they exist.

After the job is installed, you must produce two artefacts that the automated grader will inspect:

A. File: /home/user/backup/crontab_after.txt  
   • It must contain the entire resulting crontab for the current user.  
   • You must generate it **exactly** by issuing a command equivalent to  
     crontab -l > /home/user/backup/crontab_after.txt  
   so that the file is a faithful, line-for-line dump of the active crontab.  
   • The grader will search this file for the single line (verbatim, no leading/trailing spaces):  
     30 3 * * * /home/user/backup/cleanup.sh >> /home/user/backup/cleanup.log 2>&1  

B. File: /home/user/backup/verification.log  
   • Create it with precisely one line:  
     CRON JOB INSTALLED  
   • No extra spaces or trailing blank lines.

Directory layout prerequisites (already present in the lab image):  
/home/user/backup/  
 ├─ cleanup.sh        (an existing dummy script)  
 └─ (you will add files here)

What you have to do, step by step (conceptual overview – **do NOT** copy/paste these commands verbatim):

1. Open or create your personal crontab.  
2. Append the single cron line that fulfils points 1-4 above.  
3. Save/exit so it becomes active.  
4. Export the entire crontab to /home/user/backup/crontab_after.txt exactly as specified.  
5. Create /home/user/backup/verification.log with the required text.

Important output format notes the grader will enforce:

• /home/user/backup/crontab_after.txt  
  - Must exist and be a plain text file.  
  - Must contain the precise cron line, starting with ‘30 3 * * *’, followed by **one** space before the script path, and ending with ‘2>&1’.  
  - The file may contain other pre-existing lines; that is fine.

• /home/user/backup/verification.log  
  - Must exist and have a single line exactly equal to:  
    CRON JOB INSTALLED

That’s it—this is manageable with a single well-chosen terminal command plus two quick follow-up commands to generate the required files.
truth:
Expected final state for automated tests:

1. File /home/user/backup/crontab_after.txt exists.  
   • It contains, somewhere in the file, this exact line (no surrounding spaces, tabs, or extra redirection order):  
     30 3 * * * /home/user/backup/cleanup.sh >> /home/user/backup/cleanup.log 2>&1

2. File /home/user/backup/verification.log exists and its full contents are exactly one line:  
   CRON JOB INSTALLED

3. /home/user/backup/cleanup.log is **not** graded; its presence is irrelevant.

The tests will read /home/user/backup/crontab_after.txt, parse for the cron entry, and verify that the timing fields, script path and redirection segment exactly match the line above.  
They will also confirm that /home/user/backup/verification.log exists and contains the single expected line with a trailing newline optionally accepted.
